fr:mainmatter.html:p: We've now spent a few weeks looks at logics determined by matrices, and some of the ways we can use those matrices to learn about these logics. The aim this time is to think about the limits of this approach: which logics can we reach via matrices, and which can't we?
Key to this lecture is the idea of a matrix logic:
A matrix logic is a logic that is determined by some matrix.
For example, classical logic, K3, LP, and FDE are all examples of matrix logics. For each of these, we've seen a matrix that determines the logic.

Remember, though, that a matrix logic is a kind of logic, and a logic is just a set of arguments. So even when we know about a logic through some mechanism other than a matrix, it still might be a matrix logic.

For example, recall the universal logic, the logic that contains every argument. When you first met this logic, we hadn't thought about matrices at all yet, or even structures or valuations! I introduced you to this logic directly, without any detour through matrices, because it's an easy example of a set of arguments.

But despite this, the universal logic is a matrix logic. To see this, consider a one-valued structure. (There are no choices here; there is only one one-valued structure, where every connective just gives the same value back.) And consider the matrix built on this structure where the one value is designated. The logic determined by this matrix is the universal logic.
How could you know that the logic determined by this matrix is the universal logic?

Take any argument \Gamma \;/\; A. A counterexample to this argument in the matrix in question would have to be a valuation v in the one-valued structure where:
for everyA in \Gamma , the value v(B) is designated, and
v(A) is not designated. But this is impossible: there is only one value, so v(A) must be that value, which is designated in this matrix. So there are no counterexamples to any argument, and so the logic determined by this matrix is the universal logic.
So the universal logic is a matrix logic, since it is determined by some matrix.

That example helps me make another important point: although every matrix determines one particular logic, we can't in general go the other way. When a logic is a matrix logic, there is not just one matrix that determines it; a logic can be determined by many different matrices. (I won't go through the reasoning here, but in fact every matrix logic is determined by infinitely many different matrices.)

For example, consider a very different matrix: this time, take the strong Kleene structure, and consider the matrix on that structure that uses \{\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}\} as its set of designated values. This matrix also determines the universal logic, by essentially the same reasoning as before; but it is a very different matrix, built on a completely different structure.

So when we say that a logic is a matrix logic, this doesn't mean that there is any particular matrix pinned down by the logic. It just means that there is at least one matrix, somewhere out there, that determines the logic.

To set the stage for the rest of this lecture, let's also look at the empty logic, the logic that contains no arguments. This too was one of the first logics you met in these lectures. But unlike the universal logic, the empty logic is not a matrix logic. That is, there is absolutely no matrix that determines the empty logic.

At first, it might seem hard to see how we could know that. Structures themselves come in a bewildering variety, and then almost every structure underlies many different matrices. So how could we know that none of these options ever determines the empty logic?

Here's one way. Think about the argument p \;/\; p. Like all arguments, this argument is invalid in the empty logic. So if the empty logic were a matrix logic, there would be some matrix in which this argument has a counterexample.

Now I'll show, however, that that can't be the case. Imagine we have some matrix, and some valuation v on the matrix's structure, so that v is a counterexample to p \;/\; p in the matrix. What would we know about v(p)?

Well, since we're imagining that v is a counterexample to p \;/\; p, we would know that v(p), whatever it is, would have to be designated in the matrix, since p is a premise of the argument p \;/\; p. And we would also know that v(p), whatever it is, would have to be undesignated in the matrix, since p is the conclusion of the argument p \;/\; p. But no value in any matrix could ever be both designated and undesignated, so this just isn't possible. That is, there is no counterexample to p \;/\; p in any matrix.

That means, though, that the argument p \;/\; p must be valid in every matrix logic. And since this argument is invalid in the empty logic, it follows that the empty logic is not a matrix logic. This means that the techniques we learned in the last lecture that let us use matrices to explore the logics determined by those matrices, those techniques won't work to help us learn about the empty logic, since there is no matrix that determines it.

Now, the empty logic is very simple, so this isn't a big problem; we can probably answer any questions we have about the empty logic in some other way. But it reveals a bigger issue: matrices are just not flexible enough to handle all logics; only certain logics are matrix logics. This bigger issue forms the basis of the rest of this lecture, which approaches the question: exactly which logics are, and which are not, matrix logics? (We won't fully answer the question in this lecture, but we'll get close.)

To start exploring this issue, we can generalise the example from above. There was nothing special about the choice of the sentence p in the argument p \;/\; p. Take any sentence A at all, and consider the argument A \;/\; A. By the same reasoning as before, there can't be any counterexample to this argument in any matrix. (Imagine there was such a counterexample v. Then v(A) would have to be both designated and undesignated, which is impossible.)
So this means that every matrix logic has to include, not only the argument p \;/\; p, but every argument with the form A \;/\; A.
A logic is reflexive iff it validates all arguments with the form A \;/\; A.
What we have seen, then, is that every matrix logic is reflexive. That already narrows down what we can use matrices for.

Consider these three logics. Which of them is reflexive?
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is p,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is in \Gamma ,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has five or fewer members,

html:ol.html:li: This logic is not reflexive; although it does contain p \;/\; p, it does not contain (for example) q \;/\; q or p \land q \;/\; p \land q.
This logic is reflexive; in every argument of the form A \;/\; A, the conclusion is one of the premises (indeed the only premise), so all such arguments are valid in this logic.
This logic is reflexive; every argument of the form A \;/\; A has exactly one premise, so it indeed has five or fewer; so all such arguments are valid in this logic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Some logics, then, are reflexive and others are not. But as we've seen, all matrix logics must be reflexive.
As an example that we'll make some use of in a moment, note that there is a reflexive logic that's weaker than every reflexive logic; let's call this one the weakest reflexive logic, or WRL:
The weakest reflexive logic, also called WRL, is the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma = \{A\}.

The definition just tells you what WRL is; it doesn't actually show that WRL really is weaker than every reflexive logic. But you now know enough to see that it is. For a logic to be reflexive means that it contains every argument of the form A \;/\; A---but these arguments are exactly the ones valid in WRL. So every argument valid in WRL is valid in any reflexive logic; that shows that WRL is weaker than every reflexive logic.
Note also that WRL is itself reflexive: it itself contains every argument of the form A \;/\; A.

But we can go further, now asking: well, is every reflexive logic a matrix logic? This is an example of how logicians learn things: once we manage to answer one question, that answer often suggests an additional, more specific, question. Over decades (but not yet quite centuries!) of work, the global community of logicians has managed to learn a ton---but there's always more. That's why puzzles are so central to what we do; it's to give you a sense of the kind of questioning and answering that is core to the actual practice of logic.

In fact, not every reflexive logic is a matrix logic; there are other things that all matrix logics have in common, things not guaranteed by reflexivity. To see this, think about WRL. WRL is reflexive, as we've seen. But is it a matrix logic?

In fact, it isn't. Take, for example, the argument p, q \;/\; p. This is invalid in WRL, since it doesn't have the form A \;/\; A. But think about what a counterexample to p, q \;/\; p in a matrix would have to do. It would have to be some valuation v where v(p) and v(q) are both designated but v(p) is undesignated. And that means that it would have to already be a counterexample to p \;/\; p, which we've seen is impossible. So WRL isn't a matrix logic, even though it is reflexive.

The reasoning there can be generalised: if a valuation v is a counterexample to any argument \Sigma \;/\; A, and \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , then v must also be a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A. This is because if v takes every member of \Sigma to a designated value, and every member of \Gamma is a member of \Sigma , then v takes every member of \Gamma to a designated value as well.

Unlike in the case of reflexivity, then, we haven't just discovered here certain arguments that must be valid in all matrix logics. We've discovered something more nuanced: a certain connection among arguments that all matrix logics must respect in a certain way. To explore this more systematically, let me pause to introduce an idea that will come in handy both here and going forward: the idea of a rule.
To get to rules, let's start with the idea of a rule instance:

fr:title.text: Rule instance, obeying a rule instance

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Rule instance, obeying a rule instance

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A rule instance has two parts: first, a set of arguments (called the premise-arguments), and second, a particular argument (called the conclusion-argument). A rule instance is written with a horizontal line, with the premise-arguments occurring above the line and the conclusion-argument below it.

A logic obeys a rule instance if either:
the rule instance has some premise-argument that is invalid in the logic, or
the rule instance's conclusion-argument is valid in the logic.
A rule instance is kind of like an argument, except that where arguments use sentences, rule instances use arguments. So we're up a level now.

Also, although officially rule instances can have any number of premise-arguments, for this lecture we will only need to think about rule instances with one or two premise-arguments. So don't worry too much about going wild here.

You can think of a rule instance as setting a kind of condition on logics; that's what the idea of obeying a rule instance is about. I like to think about it like this: a rule instance makes a demand on every logic that contains all the rule instance's premise-arguments. In particular, it demands that all those logics must contain the conclusion-argument. A logic obeys the rule instance when either the rule instance makes no demand on the logic (since some premise-argument is invalid in the logic) or else the logic meets the demand.
For example, think about the rule instance

Which of the following logics obeys this rule instance?
the logic that contains \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has exactly one member
the logic that contains \Gamma \;/\; A iff all members of \Gamma are atomic
the logic that contains \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has three or more members

html:ol.html:li: This logic does obey the rule instance, since the conclusion-argument is valid in the logic.
This logic does not obey the rule instance, since the premise-argument is valid in the logic but the conclusion-argument is not.
This logic does obey the rule instance, since there is a premise-argument that is not valid in the logic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A rule is a set of rule instances. A logic obeys a rule iff it obeys every member of the rule.

A rule collects up a bunch of rule instances into a single package. You can think of a rule as placing a whole lot of conditions on a logic at once: one for each rule instance in it. To obey a rule, a logic has to obey all its instances.
Now that we've got the idea of a rule, let's look at our first example rule: a rule called monotonicity:
Monotonicity is the rule whose instances are those that fit the form:
where \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma .

Another way to say the same thing: a rule instance is a member of monotonicity iff:
it has one premise-argument,
the conclusion of its premise-argument matches the conclusion of its conclusion-argument, and
the set of premises of its premise-argument is a subset of the set of premises of its conclusion-argument.
A logic is monotonic iff it obeys monotonicity.

To get some practice with monotonicity, think about these logics. Which of them are monotonic?
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is p,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is in \Gamma ,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has five or fewer members,

html:ol.html:li: This logic is monotonic. Whenever we have an instance of monotonicity, the conclusions of the premise-argument and the conclusion-argument must match. If they are both p, then the conclusion-argument is valid in the logic, so the logic obeys the instance. And if neither of them is p, then the premise-argument is not valid in the logic, so the logic obeys the instance. So the logic obeys all instances of monotonicity.
This logic is monotonic, because whenever \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , then if A is in \Gamma , it must also be in \Sigma .

html:li.html:p: This logic is not monotonic; here is an instance of monotonicity that it does not obey:

fr:mainmatter.html:p: So a logic is monotonic iff, whenever it contains an argument \Gamma \;/\; A and \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma , then the logic contains \Sigma \;/\; A as well. And in fact, every matrix logic is monotonic. To show this, I'll proceed by a method called showing the contrapositive.

What I'll show directly is that, when A is a subset of \Sigma , then if a matrix logic invalidates \Sigma \;/\; A it must also invalidate \Gamma \;/\; A. What I want to show is that if \Gamma \;/\; A is valid then \Sigma \;/\; A must be valid too; what I do show both switches the order of these and changes from validity to invalidity. If this seems like a weird thing to do, just come along for the ride for now; hopefully it will make more sense in a paragraph or two.

Suppose, then, that we have a matrix logic where \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid. Since the logic is specified by a matrix, that must mean that there is some counterexample in the matrix to the argument \Sigma \;/\; A. And that, in turn, must mean that there is a valuation v that assigns a designated value to everything in \Sigma and also assigns an undesignated value to A. Now, supposing that \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma , this means that v must assign a designated value to everything in \Gamma as well, since everything in \Gamma is in \Sigma and everything in \Sigma gets a designated value. So v is also a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A, since it does what a counterexample must do: it assigns a designated value to every premise and an undesignated value to the conclusion.

I've now shown that if \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid then \Gamma \;/\; A is invalid (in any matrix logic, when \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma ). What I want, remember, is to show that if \Gamma \;/\; A is valid then so is \Sigma \;/\; A. But that follows: if \Gamma \;/\; A is valid, then it can't be that \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid---we just saw that when \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid then so is \Gamma \;/\; A. So when \Gamma \;/\; A is valid, that must mean that \Sigma \;/\; A is valid too. That's monotonicity.

Again, this doesn't depend on any details of the matrix: it doesn't depend on which structure is involved, or on which values are designated, or even on what language we're using. The key step in the reasoning is this: if a valuation takes every sentence in \Sigma to a designated value, and \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma , then the valuation takes every sentence in \Gamma to a designated value.

We've shown, then, that every matrix logic is both reflexive and monotonic. That means that lots of logics are not matrix logics! That is, for lots of logics there is no way of giving a matrix that determines them. Working with matrices is a powerful tool, but it only works for certain special logics.

But there's more; matrix logics are in fact even more restricted than this. Think about the following example logic, which I'll call L1 (we won't use it for more than a few paragraphs, so it doesn't need a snazzy name). L1 contains an argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff either:
A is a member of \Gamma , or
A is q, and p is a member of \Gamma , or
A is r, and q is a member of \Gamma .

Let me try to convince you first that L1 is reflexive and monotonic. For any argument of the form A \;/\; A, we indeed have that its conclusion is among its premises, so all these arguments are valid in L1 by the first dot point. So L1 is reflexive.

To see that L1 is monotonic, suppose we have any argument \Gamma \;/\; A that is valid in L1, and any set \Sigma such that \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma . Then \Gamma \;/\; A must fit one of the three conditions for L1, since that's the only way it could be valid in L1. But you can check each of these conditions, to see that if \Gamma \;/\; A fits the condition, then \Sigma \;/\; A must fit that same condition, so \Sigma \;/\; A must be valid in L1 as well. That shows that L1 is monotonic.

Despite its being reflexive and monotonic, however, L1 is not a matrix logic. Again, let's think about what a matrix that determines L1 would have to be like, to see that it's not possible. This time, the key facts we need are that p \;/\; q and p, q \;/\; r are both valid in L1, but p \;/\; r is not valid in L1. (Make sure you see that this is so.)

Since p \;/\; r is invalid in L1, if L1 is indeed determined by some matrix, then there would have to be some valuation v so that v(p) is designated in the matrix but v(r) is undesignated. But then what about v(q)? There are two possibilities: either v(q) is designated in the matrix or it is undesignated. But if it is designated, then v is also a counterexample to p, q \;/\; r, and so it does not determine L1. And if it is undesignated, then v is a counterexample to p \;/\; q, and so again it doesn't determine L1.
So we have found another constraint that matrix logics impose, besides reflexivity and monotonicity. There are simpler and more complex versions of this constraint; here we'll work with a simple one:
Transitivity is the rule whose instances are those that fit the form:
A logic is transitive iff it obeys transitivity.

(To make something explicit: the given general form of transitivity has \Gamma , A \;/\; B as the form of its second premise-argument. But every argument, as you know, has a single set of premises. So what is "\Gamma , A" here? You should read it as the union of \Gamma with \{A\}. That is, if A is already a member of \Gamma , then "\Gamma , A" just refers to \Gamma . And if A is not a member of \Gamma , then "\Gamma , A" refers to the set that contains everything in \Gamma , and also contains A (and contains nothing else). )
This is a more complex rule than monotonicity, since its instances have two premise-arguments each. So let's pause and think through some examples.
First, a simple one, revisiting L1. Consider the following rule instance:

This rule instance is an instance of transitivity. To see this, return to the general form of transitivity; you can see this instance as fitting into it with:
\Gamma as \{p\},
A as q,
B as r.

Thinking about this particular instance of transitivity, note that L1 does not obey it, since both premise-arguments of the instance are valid in L1 but the conclusion-argument is not. So L1 is not transitive, since there it at least one instance of transivity that it does not obey.
For another example instance of transitivity, consider this rule instance:

This also fits the general form of transivity, with:
\Gamma as \{p, q\},
A as r,
B as s.

Unlike our previous example, L1 does obey this instance, since p, q, r \;/\; s is invalid in L1. But L1 still is not transitive, since being transitive requires obeying every instance of transitivity, and we have already seen that there is an instance L1 does not obey.
As you might have guessed, I'm about to argue that every matrix logic is transitive. To see this, consider any instance of transitivity:

To show that every matrix logic must obey this instance, it is enough to show that if the conclusion-argument is invalid in a matrix logic, then one or the other premise-arguments must aslo be invalid in that matrix logic.

So take any matrix logic where \Gamma \;/\; B is invalid. This logic must be determined by some matrix where we have a valuation v that is a counterexample to this argument. That means that v(C) must be designated in the matrix for each C in \Gamma , and that v(B) must be undesignated.

But now think about v(A). Either this is designated in the matrix in question, or it is undesignated. If it is designated, then v is also a counterexample to \Gamma , A \;/\; B; and if it is undesignated, then v is also a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A. So either way, it can't be that both \Gamma \;/\; A and \Gamma , A \;/\; B are valid in the logic determined by the matrix. Thus, the logic determined by the matrix does obey this rule instance.
And since any instance of transitivity fits this form, that means that every matrix logic must obey all such instances. That is, all matrix logics are transitive.

I mentioned above that transitivity comes in many forms, some simpler and some more complex. The one I look at in the main thread of the lecture is a particularly simple one; I've chosen it for being easier to understand than some others. (That doesn't mean it's easy!) But in fact there are some other forms of transitivity that are more closely connected to what's really happening with matrices. In this side note, I'll look at one of the most important. This is totally skippable, though; it's just for if you find yourself interested!

The key is to note that the same reasoning about transitivity given above can apply to lots of sentences at the same time, not just a single sentence A. So suppose you have some matrix counterexample to some argument \Gamma \;/\; A. Then consider any set \Sigma of sentences. Either the counterexample you have assigns a designated value to every sentence in \Sigma , or it assigns an undesignated value to at least one member of \Sigma .

If it assigns a designated value to every sentence in \Sigma , then it is also a counterexample to \Gamma , \Sigma \;/\; A. And if it assigns an undesignated value to some member B of \Sigma , then it is a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; B.

This means that, for any matrix logic, any sets \Gamma and \Sigma , and any sentence A, if \Gamma \;/\; B is valid for all B in \Sigma , and if \Gamma , \Sigma \;/\; A is also valid, then \Gamma \;/\; A must be valid as well. This gives us a different rule than the one I've called 'transitivity' above. How many premise-arguments an instance of this rule has depends on how many members \Sigma has for that instance. In particular, there are instances of this rule with infinitely many premise-arguments.

That's important; there are logics that are transitive (in this lecture's sense of 'transitive') but that do not obey this rule, because of these infinite instances. Basically, transitivity just requires that we can do this when \Sigma has one member. And by repeating the reasoning, that turns out to be enough to show that we can do it wheneven \Sigma has finitely many members. But to make the leap to the infinite instances really is something more, something that does not follow from transitivity.

For consideration of a wider range of different transitivity properties, you can also see my paper "On the 'transitivity' of consequence relations"; like almost all my papers, this is on my website at https://negation.rocks/writing.html. That paper talks about the rule I'm calling 'transitivity' here under the name \text {F}_{\text {SF}}, and the stronger rule I'm discussing in this side note under the name \text {C}_{\text {SF}}.
Finally, let's return to the idea of a substitution. Here's a rule, called substitution invariance:
Substitution invariance is the rule whose instances are those that fit the form:

where there is some substitution s so that:
\Sigma is the set of results you get by applying s to each sentence in \Gamma , and
s(A) = B.
A logic is substitution invariant iff it obeys substitution invariance.

So when a logic is substitution invariant, if we have an argument valid in the logic, we can take any substitution we like, apply it to that argument, and get a (potentially) new argument that is also valid in the logic.
For example, the universal and empty logics are both substitution invariant, since neither of them has any cases where a substitution could take us from a valid argument to an invalid one.
More interestingly, though, every matrix logic is substitution invariant. Let's see why. We'll start by thinking about classical logic, but the strategy we'll use is general.

First, think about the argument p \land q, r \lor s \;/\; p \land s, and a substitution f where:
f(p) = \top
f(q) = p \land q
f(r) = t \rightarrow s
f(s) = s Then the substitution instance of the argument we need to think about is \top \land (p \land q), (t \rightarrow s) \lor s \;/\; \top \land s. That is, because of the substitution f, the following is an instance of substitution invariance:

Now, let's think about whether classical logic obeys this instance. But let's do it in a certain way that will reveal an idea: let's start by looking at the conclusion-argument. Is this argument classically valid?

Now, we can use this valuation v and the substitution f to reach a valuation u that is a counterexample to the premise-argument, as follows. For any atomic sentence A, we let u(A) = v(f(A)). Here's how this works:
since f(p) = \top and v(\top ) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we let u(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}},
since f(q) = p \land q and v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we let u(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}},
since f(r) = t \rightarrow s and v(t \rightarrow s) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we let u(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
since f(s) = s and v(s) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, we let u(s) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

Now, note that u must be a counterexample to the premise-argument. The reason is that, for every atomic sentence in this premise-argument, u gives it a particular value: the value that its f-substitution instance receives in the conclusion-argument. Whatever those values are, we already know that they come together in the form given to produce a counterexample to that conclusion-argument. So that must happen in the premise-argument as well.

Although this is just one example, it illustrates a general phenomenon. The key features of matrices that make this work are:
For any way of assigning any values to any atomic sentences, there is always a valuation that does that. This is what we need to be sure that there is always some valuation like u, no matter which valuation like v we start with. If, for example, there were some values that atomic sentences could not take, but that compound sentences could take, this reasoning would no longer work. But we know, from what valuations are, that this isn't so.
The value assigned by a valuation to a compound sentence depends only on its main connective and the values assigned to its components. This is what we need to be sure that all the sentences in the premise-argument take the same value on u that their substitution instances in the conclusion-argument take on v. We are making sure that the values of all the atomic sentences in the premise-argument do this, but we need to be sure that that stays true for all the sentences that serve as premises or conclusions. So we need to know that the value of a compound sentence can't depend on anything else. But that also comes from what valuations are.
Finally, we need to know that whether a valuation is a counterexample to an argument in a matrix depends only on the values that that valuation assigns to sentences in the argument. That way, after we've made sure that u assigns all the same values to the premise-argument's sentences that v assigned to the conclusion-argument's sentences, we can know that, since v is a counterexample to the conclusion-argument, u must be a counterexample to the premise-argument. And this is something else we do know, now from what a counterexample in a matrix is.

With those three ingredients in place, everything else works out. Nothing in particular mattered in the above reasoning about how many values we have in our structure, or which of those values are designated in our matrix, or what the connectives do to the values in the structure. So whenever we have a matrix logic and an instance of substitution invariance, the same reasoning will work to show that the logic obeys the instance. Thus, all matrix logics are substitution invariant.

This lecture focussed on the limits of matrix methods: what kinds of logics we can determine via matrices, and so also what kinds we can't. The key ideas are the idea of a matrix logic, the idea of a rule, and the properties of reflexivity, monotonicity, transitivity, and substitution invariance. As we've seen, every matrix logic has all four of these properties. That means that matrix methods are harder to use (and usually not the right idea at all) when exploring logics that lack one or more of these properties.

Recall four properties that a logic might or might not have: reflexivity, monotonicity, transitivity, and substitution invariance. We've seen that every logic given by a matrix has all four of these properties. What about logics given by natural deduction systems?

First, think about reflexivity. As long as we allow ourselves to assume any sentence at all, then we will always have a proof of A \;/\; A, no matter what sentence A is: just the assumption of A constitutes such a proof. So reflexivity is assured. If we were to work with a system with restrictions on what could be assumed, then there would be a question about reflexivity. But we haven't looked at any such systems.

Second, think about monotonicity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. Then that proof itself is also a proof of \Sigma \;/\; A for any \Sigma where \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma . This is because of how we've defined what it is to be a proof of an argument. So any logic determined by a proof system in the way we're doing it here will be monotonic.

Third, think about transitivity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A and a proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B. The question is: given that much, must we always have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B? Yes, we must; here's why. Consider the proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B---call this proof P---and think about its open assumptions. We don't know what these are, but we do know that each of them must either be in \Gamma or be A. If they're all in \Gamma , we're all set: the proof P is already also a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, which is what we're after.

But they might not all be in \Gamma . In particular, there can also be open assumptions of A in the proof P, and A might not be in \Gamma . If this is the case, then P is not yet a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. But we can make use of the other proof we've assumed we have: the one of \Gamma \;/\; A.

Imagine plugging a copy of this other proof of \Gamma \;/\; A in at the top of P, wherever there is an open assumption of A. So instead of assuming A in P, we'll instead prove it via the proof of \Gamma \;/\; A we have. If we do this for every open assumption of A in P, we'll end up with a new larger proof of B, and now one where all its open assumptions are in \Gamma . That is, we'll have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B. So the logics determined by our natural deduction proof systems are transitive.

That was notably more complex than reflexivity or monotonicity, so let's look at an example to see how it works. It's important to recall, though, that just one example isn't enough to show that transitivity holds. This needs to work for every possible example. That's why the schematic and general reasoning above was important. This one example is just to give the flavour; but you should look over it while keeping an eye on the general reasoning, to convince yourself this kind of thing is indeed always possible.
This example uses only rules from NM, so it works in any of our three systems. Here are two simple proofs in NM:

The first of these is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; q, and the second of p \rightarrow q, p, r, q \;/\; r \land q. So if the logic this system determines is transitive, there had better be a proof somewhere in the system of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q. And indeed there is: we take the first example proof here, and plug it into the top of the second, replacing each open assumption of q in the second proof with a copy of the first proof. The result looks like this:

See how, in this result, the bottom is made up of the second example proof above, while the top bits are filled in with the first proof. This result is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q; just what we needed.

The key to making sure this is possible is this: all of our rules for building larger proofs out of smaller proofs (which is to say, all of our rules other than assumption, \top I, and LEM) are always applicable based only on the conclusions of the smaller proofs they build on. In the above example, the rule \land I doesn't care whether its inputs are assumptions, as they are in the original example, or proofs that do actual work, as they are in the finished product. The rule \land I acts on them just the same either way. So when we plug in full proofs for open assumptions, we can leave everything else alone and be confident that it will all work out: the result will still be a proof.

It's worth pausing here to think through how this would look with rules that do some discharging hanging around in the proofs. I promise it will work! But it is worth thinking through an example or two.

Finally, substitution invariance. This one is more straightforward: applying a substitution to an entire proof always results in another proof. So whenever we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, if we want a proof of some substitution instance of that argument, we can just apply the appropriate substitution to the proof itself, and we're all set. To be sure this works, you need to check that it works for every rule. After all, imagine we had a rule like this:

Think about adding this rule to NJ. Then we reach a proof system that does include a proof of p \lor q \;/\; p. But this proof system still does not include any proof of r \lor s \;/\; r. The reason is that the rule FAKE RULE is not itself substitution invariant: it applies only to the particular sentences that occur in it. The actual rules of NM, NJ, and NK, though, are not like that. Every actual rule in these systems applies to every substitution instance of anything it applies to. So the logics these systems determine are indeed substitution invariant.

html:ul.html:li:
Section A: Preliminaries
Lecture 1: Introduction and languages
Lecture 2: Functions and substitutions
Lecture 3: Sets, arguments, and logics

Section B: Structures and matrices
Lecture 4: Structures
Lecture 5: Matrices and counterexamples
Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics
Lecture 7: The limits of matrices

Section C: Exploring intuitionistic logic
Lecture 8: Natural deduction
Lecture 9: Intuitionistic logic
Lecture 10: Intuitionistic models
Lecture 11: Harmony
